content.text: (REC) is a CloudFront caching tier that sits between the 600+ Edge Locations (PoPs) and the AWS Region origin. There are 13 RECs distributed globally, each with roughly

of an individual PoP. When a PoP experiences a cache miss, it checks the nearest REC before forwarding the request to the origin, absorbing a second layer of cache hits without the latency or cost of an origin round-trip.

attrs.alt: Three-tier CloudFront hierarchy: Edge Location PoPs -> Regional Edge Caches -> AWS Region origin

content.text: Individual Edge PoPs are optimized for speed, not storage. Their SSD caches are limited in size and evict objects on a least-recently-used basis. Popular objects stay warm at the PoP;
(objects requested by a small number of users in each city) gets evicted quickly. Without RECs, every long-tail miss goes directly to origin, creating:
: A video file requested once per city from 600 cities = 600 simultaneous origin fetches. With a REC, it is fetched once per REC (13 fetches) and then served locally to all PoPs in that region.
: An object not in the PoP cache but in the REC is served in 20-70 ms rather than 80-250 ms from an origin in another continent.
: Each origin fetch generates CloudFront-to-origin data transfer charges. RECs reduce the number of origin fetches proportionally to their hit rate.
- you do not configure them explicitly. When you create a CloudFront distribution, all 13 RECs are automatically used unless you enable Origin Shield or set cache TTLs to zero.
AWS operates 13 Regional Edge Caches. Their approximate geographic coverage:
Cache Hierarchy: How a Request Flows
Understanding the exact flow reveals where each optimization applies:
: DNS resolves the CloudFront domain to the nearest PoP via Anycast routing. The PoP checks its local SSD cache.
: Response served directly from SSD in under 5-20 ms. Most common outcome for popular content (60-80% of requests).
: The PoP makes a request to the nearest REC traversing the AWS backbone (not the public internet). The REC checks its 12x larger cache.
: The REC serves the response to the PoP, which caches it locally. RTT = REC-to-PoP latency (5-30 ms within the same continent) plus PoP-to-user.
: The REC fetches from the origin (S3, ALB, etc.), caches the response, and forwards it back through the chain. This is the most expensive path.
The combined hit rate of PoP and REC means that only
typically reach the origin for well-configured distributions with appropriate TTLs.
What RECs Cache vs. What They Skip
RECs cache the same objects that PoPs cache, subject to the same cache-control headers and CloudFront TTL settings. Several object types bypass both tiers:
Long TTL; ideal for both tiers
REC stores long-tail segments PoP evicted
Expiry in REC same as PoP
Only GET and HEAD are cacheable by default
Cannot serve same cached copy to different clients
The REC cache hit rate is dominated by
- how long objects stay in the cache before being considered stale. A TTL of zero means every request is a miss at every tier. A long TTL keeps objects warm across the full hierarchy:
Origin Shield: Collapsing REC Traffic to One
By default, all 13 RECs can make independent origin fetches for the same object if they all miss simultaneously.
adds a fourth caching layer in front of the origin: a single designated REC acts as the sole gateway, collapsing all cross-REC misses into
Origin request volume on cache miss
High burst for popular new content

# Enable Origin Shield on a CloudFront origin
# Specify the AWS Region closest to your origin server
aws cloudfront get-distribution-config --id "$DIST_ID" > /tmp/dist-config.json
ETAG=$(jq -r '.ETag' /tmp/dist-config.json)

# Update origin to enable Origin Shield
jq '.DistributionConfig.Origins.Items[0].OriginShield = {"Enabled": true, "OriginShieldRegion": "us-east-1"}' \
  /tmp/dist-config.json | jq '.DistributionConfig' > /tmp/updated-dist.json

aws cloudfront update-distribution \
  --id "$DIST_ID" \
  --distribution-config file:///tmp/updated-dist.json \
  --if-match "$ETAG" \
  --query 'Distribution.{Status:Status}'

# Request path with Origin Shield enabled (four tiers):
# User -> PoP -> REC -> Origin Shield REC (us-east-1) -> S3/ALB
Cache Key Normalization: Maximizing REC Hit Rates
A common reason for low REC hit rates is

: the same logical object cached multiple times with different keys because tracking parameters, session tokens, or irrelevant headers differ between requests. CloudFront cache policies control which request attributes contribute to the cache key:
Monitoring: Measuring REC vs. PoP Hit Rates
metric in CloudWatch, but it does not break down hits by tier. To distinguish PoP hits, REC hits, and origin misses, enable CloudFront real-time logs and analyze the
Object served from PoP SSD cache (Tier 1 hit)
PoP revalidated with origin using If-None-Match; got 304 Not Modified
PoP missed; either REC or origin served (must check x-edge-response-result-type for detail)
Origin Shield REC served the response without contacting origin
4xx or 5xx from origin, forwarded through the hierarchy

import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

cw = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1')
DIST_ID = 'E1ABCDEF1234567'

end = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
start = end - timedelta(hours=24)

resp = cw.get_metric_statistics(
    Namespace='AWS/CloudFront',
    MetricName='CacheHitRate',
    Dimensions=[
        {'Name': 'DistributionId', 'Value': DIST_ID},
        {'Name': 'Region', 'Value': 'Global'},
    ],
    StartTime=start, EndTime=end,
    Period=3600, Statistics=['Average'],
)

rates = sorted(resp['Datapoints'], key=lambda d: d['Timestamp'])
for pt in rates:
    t = pt['Timestamp'].strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')
    r = pt['Average']
    print(f'{t}  cache hit rate: {r:.1f}%')

# For per-tier analysis, query CloudFront access logs in S3 using Athena:
# SELECT
#   x_edge_result_type,
#   COUNT(*) as requests,
#   ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER(), 1) as pct
# FROM cloudfront_logs
# WHERE year='2024' AND month='01' AND day='15'
# GROUP BY x_edge_result_type
# ORDER BY requests DESC
Cost Impact of REC Hit Rate
Each origin fetch from a REC incurs data-transfer charges from origin to CloudFront. RECs hit rates directly control origin bandwidth costs:

# Rough cost model for a distribution with 1 TB/day viewer traffic
GB_PER_DAY = 1000
VIEWER_TRANSFER_RATE = 0.0085   # USD/GB (US/EU pricing, first 10 TB)
ORIGIN_TRANSFER_RATE = 0.020    # USD/GB (origin in us-east-1 to CloudFront)

# Scenario A: 15% of traffic reaches origin (poor REC optimization)
origin_pct_a = 0.15
cost_a = GB_PER_DAY * VIEWER_TRANSFER_RATE + GB_PER_DAY * origin_pct_a * ORIGIN_TRANSFER_RATE
print(f"Scenario A (15% origin): viewer=${GB_PER_DAY * VIEWER_TRANSFER_RATE:.0f} + "
      f"origin=${GB_PER_DAY * origin_pct_a * ORIGIN_TRANSFER_RATE:.0f} = ${cost_a:.0f}/day")

# Scenario B: 5% of traffic reaches origin (versioned URLs + long TTL)
origin_pct_b = 0.05
cost_b = GB_PER_DAY * VIEWER_TRANSFER_RATE + GB_PER_DAY * origin_pct_b * ORIGIN_TRANSFER_RATE
print(f"Scenario B ( 5% origin): viewer=${GB_PER_DAY * VIEWER_TRANSFER_RATE:.0f} + "
      f"origin=${GB_PER_DAY * origin_pct_b * ORIGIN_TRANSFER_RATE:.0f} = ${cost_b:.0f}/day")

monthly_savings = (cost_a - cost_b) * 30
print(f"Monthly savings from improved REC hit rate: ${monthly_savings:.0f}")
# Expected output:
# Scenario A (15% origin): viewer=8 + origin=3 = $11/day
# Scenario B ( 5% origin): viewer=8 + origin=1 = $9/day
# Monthly savings from improved REC hit rate: $60
Best Practices for Maximising REC Efficiency
These practices push aggregate hit rates above 90% for static content, letting RECs absorb the vast majority of traffic before it reaches the origin:
Use versioned URLs for static assets
: /app.a3f9b.js instead of /app.js. Set Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable. Never invalidate - update the HTML reference instead.
: Cache-Control: max-age=60 for index.html so users get new asset fingerprints quickly, while all other assets cache for a year.
: Strip tracking parameters (UTM codes, session IDs) from the cache key with a CloudFront cache policy. /img.jpg?utm_source=email and /img.jpg should hit the same cached object.
Enable Brotli and gzip in cache policy
: Set EnableAcceptEncodingBrotli and EnableAcceptEncodingGzip to true so CloudFront caches compressed variants separately, reducing bandwidth.
: This prevents caching entirely. Use query parameters for content negotiation instead of HTTP headers.
Enable Origin Shield for fragile origins
: If origin hit rate is above 20%, Origin Shield reduces concurrent origin requests from up to 13 (one per REC) to 1.
Every CloudFront response carries diagnostic headers that reveal which cache tier served the request. The
headers expose the PoP code; the
header shows how long the object has lived in cache.

# First request — likely a cache MISS at PoP, forwarded to REC then origin
curl -sI https://d1234abcd.cloudfront.net/video/intro.mp4 | grep -iE 'x-cache|x-amz-cf-pop|age|cache-control'
# X-Cache: Miss from cloudfront
# X-Amz-Cf-Pop: IAD89-P3
# Age: 0

# Second request from same PoP — PoP HIT (object cached at edge)
curl -sI https://d1234abcd.cloudfront.net/video/intro.mp4 | grep -iE 'x-cache|x-amz-cf-pop|age'
# X-Cache: Hit from cloudfront
# X-Amz-Cf-Pop: IAD89-P3
# Age: 42

# Request from distant PoP (same REC region) — RefreshHit means REC served it
curl -sI https://d1234abcd.cloudfront.net/video/intro.mp4 | grep -iE 'x-cache|x-amz-cf-pop'
# X-Cache: RefreshHit from cloudfront
# X-Amz-Cf-Pop: ORD58-C4
# The 'RefreshHit' value distinguishes a REC hit from a full origin fetch
Served from the PoP's local cache
PoP missed; REC had the object
PoP and REC both missed; origin fetched
Response too large for edge cache (>20 GB)
Bypassing RECs for Real-Time Dynamic Content
RECs only help for cacheable content. For personalized or real-time responses — user dashboards, live scores, authenticated API calls — caching is counterproductive. Use
or a CloudFront cache policy with zero TTL to ensure direct origin fetch.
: CloudFront evaluates them at the PoP level — auth happens at the edge, not at the REC.
: fires after REC miss; useful for request routing logic without bypassing caching entirely.
: when a primary origin returns 5xx, CloudFront retries the secondary — this failover path also bypasses REC cached error responses.
S3 Transfer Acceleration vs CloudFront RECs
Both services use AWS's private backbone to accelerate transfers, but they serve opposite traffic directions and use cases.
Intermediate cache layer between PoP and S3 origin
Routes PUT/POST through nearest PoP to S3 via backbone
Yes — objects cached for TTL duration
Extra per-GB fee on top of S3 PUT pricing

# Enable Transfer Acceleration on an S3 bucket
aws s3api put-bucket-accelerate-configuration \
  --bucket my-upload-bucket \
  --accelerate-configuration Status=Enabled

# Upload using the accelerated endpoint
aws s3 cp large-dataset.tar.gz \
  s3://my-upload-bucket/datasets/ \
  --endpoint-url https://my-upload-bucket.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com
A CloudFront invalidation removes objects from
PoPs and RECs simultaneously. You cannot target a specific REC — invalidation is a distribution-wide operation.

# Invalidate a specific path across all PoPs and RECs
aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
  --distribution-id EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE \
  --paths '/images/*' '/css/main.css'

# Wait for the invalidation to complete
aws cloudfront wait invalidation-completed \
  --distribution-id EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE \
  --id I2J0I21PCUYOIK

# Check invalidation status
aws cloudfront get-invalidation \
  --distribution-id EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE \
  --id I2J0I21PCUYOIK \
  --query 'Invalidation.Status'
First 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free; subsequent paths cost $0.005 each.
Wildcard paths (e.g. /images/*) count as one path but invalidate all matching objects.
Prefer versioned URLs (main.abc123.css) over invalidations in high-frequency deployment pipelines — invalidations have eventual consistency propagation time of ~10–30 seconds.
Origin Shield does NOT reduce invalidation traffic — invalidations always propagate from the CloudFront control plane to all PoPs and RECs.
Terraform: Configuring REC and Origin Shield
Infrastructure-as-code lets you pin Origin Shield and cache behavior settings repeatably across environments.
RECs are the transparent middle tier
- 13 worldwide, each with 12x PoP cache capacity, automatically activated for every CloudFront distribution.
that PoPs evict due to limited SSD space, reducing origin fetches by 15-25% on top of PoP hit rates.
collapses all REC-to-origin traffic through one designated REC region, cutting concurrent origin requests from up to 13 to 1.
cache hit rate at each tier
depends almost entirely on TTL settings and cache key normalization - versioned URLs with long TTLs are the highest-leverage lever.
in CloudFront access logs to distinguish PoP hits, REC hits, and origin fetches, then optimize TTL to shift traffic up the hierarchy.
